claim: The US Navy believes it can enforce a near-total blockade of Iranian ports indefinitely, with sufficient assets and rules of engagement to stop all oil tankers, including those under non-Iranian flags, from transiting the Strait of Hormuz.
beliefs: Blockade is legally and operationally sustainable
Iranian mining is manageable with current mine-countermeasure capabilities
Global oil market disruption is acceptable for strategic objectives
goals: Prevent any oil tanker from reaching Iranian ports
Maintain blockade until Iran capitulates or regime change occurs
commitments: Interdict and divert all tankers attempting to transit
Escort allied vessels only if necessary
pressures: Domestic political pressure to avoid escalation with Iran; allied pressure to allow humanitarian or neutral shipping
stances: No tolerance for Iranian oil exports
distinguishing_observations: US Navy stops and boards tankers daily
action_if_state: Continue aggressive interdiction, possibly expand boarding operations and use of force against non-compliant tankers.
assumptions: Iran cannot effectively retaliate against US naval assets
Mines are not a significant threat

claim: The US Navy is unable or unwilling to maintain a full blockade due to resource constraints, political pressure, or fear of escalation, leading to gaps in enforcement that allow some tankers to slip through.
beliefs: Blockade is costly and unsustainable
Risk of direct conflict with Iran is too high
Global oil prices are causing domestic backlash
goals: Reduce military footprint in the region
Avoid direct confrontation with Iranian fast boats or mines
commitments: Only interdict tankers clearly heading to Iranian ports
Allow passage of non-Iranian tankers with non-Iranian cargo
pressures: Domestic economic pressure from high oil prices; allied diplomatic pressure to ease blockade
historical_case_refs: US reduction of forces in Iraq (2011)
distinguishing_observations: US Navy does not stop all tankers
action_if_state: Reduce patrols, allow some tankers to transit, focus on monitoring rather than interdiction.
assumptions: Iran will not exploit gaps to escalate

claim: The IRGC has extensively mined the Strait of Hormuz and is actively attacking any tanker attempting to transit, using anti-ship missiles, fast boats, and drones, effectively closing the strait to all commercial traffic.
beliefs: Mining and attacks are effective deterrent
US Navy cannot clear mines quickly
Global pressure will force US to back down
goals: Deny use of strait to all shipping
commitments: Attack any vessel entering the strait
Maintain minefields and replenish as needed
pressures: Internal pressure from hardliners to maintain closure; economic pressure from lack of oil revenue
historical_case_refs: Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping (2023-2024)
action_if_state: Continue mining and attacking any tanker that approaches, possibly escalate to attacks on US Navy ships.
assumptions: Mines are not cleared by US

claim: The IRGC has limited mining capability and is not actively attacking tankers, either due to resource constraints, fear of overwhelming US retaliation, or internal political divisions, allowing some tankers to transit safely.
beliefs: Full closure is unsustainable
Selective attacks are more effective
Avoiding US retaliation is paramount
goals: Harass but not completely block shipping
commitments: Only attack tankers clearly linked to US or Israel
Avoid attacking neutral or non-aligned tankers
pressures: Internal pressure from moderates to allow some oil exports; external pressure from Russia or China
historical_case_refs: Iran's restraint after US strike on Soleimani (2020)
action_if_state: Allow some tankers to pass, focus on intelligence gathering and occasional harassment of US-allied vessels.
assumptions: US will not attack Iran if attacks are limited

claim: Tanker operators believe the strait is safe enough to transit, either because they trust US Navy escort, believe Iranian threats are bluffs, or are willing to accept high risk due to extreme profit incentives from high oil prices.
beliefs: US Navy can protect them
Iran will not attack neutral tankers
Insurance and war risk premiums are manageable
goals: Maximize profits by delivering oil to market
commitments: Transit strait with or without escort
pressures: Shareholder pressure to generate revenue; charterer demands for delivery
historical_case_refs: Tanker operations during Red Sea crisis (2023-2024)
action_if_state: Send tankers through strait in large numbers, possibly in convoys or with naval escort.

claim: Tanker operators believe the strait is too dangerous to transit due to mines, attacks, or blockade, and are unwilling to send tankers through regardless of profit potential, leading to continued very low traffic.
beliefs: Iran will attack any tanker
US Navy cannot guarantee safety
Insurance is unavailable or prohibitively expensive
goals: Avoid loss of vessel and crew
commitments: Wait for clear safe passage guarantee
pressures: Crew unions refusing to sail; charterers canceling orders; insurers denying coverage
historical_case_refs: Tanker avoidance of Persian Gulf during Tanker War (1987)
distinguishing_observations: Tanker traffic remains below 10 per day
action_if_state: Keep tankers at anchor outside strait or reroute around Africa, accept longer voyage times.

claim: Tanker operators are willing to transit only under specific conditions: if they are flagged to a neutral country, if they have prior clearance from Iran, or if they are part of a US-led escort program, leading to moderate but not full traffic.
beliefs: Some tankers are safer than others
Iran selectively targets US-linked vessels
Escort reduces risk but not to zero
goals: Transit only when risk is acceptable
Maintain relationships with both US and Iran
commitments: Only transit with neutral flag and crew
pressures: Charterers demanding delivery; insurers requiring specific conditions
historical_case_refs: Shipping to Libya during civil war (2011)
distinguishing_observations: Some tankers transit, but not enough to reach 50 per day
action_if_state: Send a limited number of tankers under specific conditions, but not enough to reach 50 per day.
assumptions: Iran does not attack neutral vessels
transition_triggers: Change in US or Iranian policy
